summary: My great-grandfather, Alexander Mitchell, was a significant financial figure in the late 1860s in Wisconsin. He was the organizer and first president of the American Bankers' Association and also played a key role in founding the Chicago, Milwaukee, St. Paul and Pacific Railroad. Additionally, he was the first president of the Northeastern National Insurance Company. Although I know little personally about him due to lost family papers, his financial legacy is well documented.
cues: Chicago, Milwaukee, St. Paul and Pacific Railroad

summary: The American Bankers Association was founded by my great-grandfather, Alexander Mitchell, who served as its first president. This organization was one of the early national institutions connected to banking in the United States, dating back to the late 19th century. Although I work in banking myself, I have always admired the role the ABA played in shaping the industry. It’s a proud part of my family’s history, even if I have not been directly involved with it.

entity: Chicago, Milwaukee, St. Paul and Pacific Railroad
summary: My great-grandfather was an organizer and stockholder of the Chicago, Milwaukee, St. Paul and Pacific Railroad. This railroad was a major enterprise in the Midwest during his time, and his involvement highlights his influence in both finance and transportation. Though I have no personal stories about his work there, it is part of the legacy I carry. It’s impressive to think about his role in such a significant company during the 19th century.

Alexander Mitchell, my great-grandfather, was the organizer and first president of the Northeastern National Insurance Company, established in 1869. This company was part of his wide-ranging financial activities in the late 1800s. While I have no direct connection to the company today, it is part of the financial heritage I come from. His leadership in this insurance firm complements his other roles in banking and railroads.

For a long time, I kept a newspaper clipping of a write-up about Villa Alexandria by Harriet Beecher Stowe, but unfortunately, I have misplaced it and cannot find it now. Stowe’s mention of the Villa was something I treasured as a connection to my family’s past. Even though I no longer have the clipping, it remains a notable part of my family’s history that ties us to that place.

Wisconsin is where my great-grandfather, Alexander Mitchell, made his mark as a financial power in the late 1860s. Although I have little personal knowledge of the family’s early days there due to lost papers, it was the state where our family first settled and prospered. Milwaukee, in particular, was central to his activities. Wisconsin represents the roots of my family’s financial legacy.

Milwaukee was the city where my great-grandfather, Alexander Mitchell, and my great-grandmother settled after arriving in the United States. It was the base for many of his financial ventures, including his involvement with the railroad and banking institutions. Unfortunately, much of our family’s documentation from that time has been lost, but Milwaukee remains a significant place in our family history. It’s where the Mitchell legacy began in earnest.

I currently live and work in Jacksonville, Florida, where I am connected with the oldest banking institution in the city. My father, David Mitchell, is also associated with Jacksonville, though I have not seen him for over a year and am unsure if he is still here. Jacksonville is where I have made my home with my mother and her family, and it is where I am trying to build my own career in banking. The city represents both my present and the place where I strive to make my mark.

I graduated from the University of Florida after attending public schools in Duval County. My education there laid the foundation for my career in banking and finance. While working full-time, I continue to study finance through programs like those offered by the American Institute of Banking. The University of Florida was a crucial step in my professional development here in Jacksonville.

I am currently studying a special course in finance through the American Institute of Banking to improve my knowledge and skills for my banking career. This education takes up much of my free time outside of work at the bank. Although I am busy, I believe continuous learning is essential to succeed in this demanding field. The Institute has been a valuable resource for me as I work to establish myself professionally.

I admire President Franklin D. Roosevelt for his ideals and his efforts to improve the finances, labor conditions, and living standards of the American people. Though I am not involved in politics myself, I respect his leadership and hope that his vision will continue to uplift the country. His presidency represents hope for progress during challenging times.

Mr. Bainbridge Richardson is a realtor in Jacksonville who might have information about my father, David Mitchell, whom I have not seen for some time. He works in the Blum Building downtown and has been helpful in confirming some family details. Though I have not spoken with him extensively, he is one of the few people who might know about my father's whereabouts. I appreciate his assistance in this personal matter.

Rose Shepherd is the writer who interviewed me and others about the Mitchell family history and banking connections in Jacksonville. She has been instrumental in documenting my story and the legacy of my family. Through her work, I have been able to share my experiences and the challenges I face in carrying on the Mitchell name. I am grateful for her interest and professionalism.

Villa Alexandria was a notable family property mentioned in connection with my ancestors. I once had a newspaper clipping by Harriet Beecher Stowe describing the Villa, but I have since lost it. The Villa holds a place in our family lore, especially related to my grandfather David Mitchell’s childhood. It was on the grounds of Villa Alexandria that he suffered a serious accident as a young man, which affected his later life.

David Mitchell was my father and the son of Alexander Mitchell’s line. He was the 'apple of his grandmother’s eye' and was given every advantage in education and travel. Unfortunately, he suffered a severe injury from a fall on the grounds of Villa Alexandria, which impacted his reliability and behavior later on. I have not seen him for over a year and do not know his current whereabouts, but he remains a significant figure in my family story.
